ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002187 (beef noodle soup) is the closest parent; this record is a set meal including chicken leg and a beverage, which is not individually coded in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM (noodle soup, beef-based) is the closest defensible parent code. The set meal components (chicken leg, beverage) are not individually represented; composite meal coding is not available in FoodEx2 at this specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Lanzhou beef noodle set meal or its composite components at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: beverage (type unspecified — likely Chinese tea or soft drink)

serving.description: 1 set meal (bowl of beef noodles + 1 chicken leg + 1 drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal consisting of approximately 400 g of Lanzhou beef noodle soup (noodles, broth, beef slices, and garnishes), one braised or roasted chicken leg (approximately 200 g), and one beverage (approximately 150 ml), totalling roughly 750 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Beef noodle soup (SR Legacy 06016), Chicken leg roasted (SR Legacy 05170), and Chinese tea / soft drink components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted combination of: (1) USDA SR Legacy 06016 'Soup, beef noodle, canned, condensed' as proxy for the noodle soup component (weight ~53% of meal), (2) USDA SR Legacy 05170 'Chicken, leg, meat and skin, cooked, roasted' as proxy for the chicken leg component (weight ~27% of meal), and (3) a generic unsweetened Chinese tea / water beverage proxy (weight ~20% of meal, negligible macros). Weighted average per 100 g of the combined 750 g set meal is reported. Values should be treated as estimates pending direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific dish.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, boiled — GI 52 (glucose reference); analogous estimate applied to hand-pulled wheat noodles in broth
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lanzhou beef noodle set meal. GI of 52 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles / instant noodles (GI ~52, low category, glucose reference). Hand-pulled wheat noodles (la mian) have a similar starch structure and cooking method; the broth and protein components further moderate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 79.5 g carbohydrate in 750 g serving at 10.6 g/100 g) / 100 ≈ 41.3, rounded to 41.9 accounting for the full composite meal carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef bone broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this set meal due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 390 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,925 mg per full 750 g serving), which substantially exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg sodium; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only a portion of the soup is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index wheat noodles with high total carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that while the GI of hand-pulled wheat noodles is moderate-to-low (~52), the large serving size delivers approximately 79.5 g of carbohydrate per set meal, resulting in a high glycemic load (~41.9); portion control, choosing a smaller noodle portion, and pairing with the protein-rich chicken leg to blunt postprandial glucose rise are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from chicken skin and beef, combined with high sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this set meal due to its combined saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g, approximately 10.5 g per serving) and high sodium content (~2,925 mg per serving); removing chicken skin, requesting less broth, and choosing a low-sugar beverage option can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk from this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lanzhou Beef Noodles with Chicken Leg and Drink is a Chinese set meal originating from Lanzhou, the capital of Gansu Province in northwestern China, and widely popularised across mainland China and Southeast Asia. The set meal consists of three components: (1) a bowl of Lanzhou-style hand-pulled beef noodle soup (兰州牛肉拉面), featuring freshly hand-pulled wheat noodles (la mian) served in a slow-simmered clear beef bone broth seasoned with spices, topped with thin slices of braised beef, white radish, chilli oil, spring onion, and coriander; (2) one braised or roasted chicken leg, providing a substantial protein accompaniment; and (3) a beverage, typically Chinese tea or a soft drink. The noodles are characteristically smooth, chewy, and elastic due to the hand-pulling technique, and the broth is savoury, mildly spiced, and aromatic. In Singapore, this dish is served at specialist Lanzhou noodle restaurants such as Lan Ting Xu (兰亭序). Nutritionally, the set meal is protein-rich (approximately 61.5 g protein per serving from beef and chicken), moderate in fat, and provides a significant carbohydrate load from the wheat noodles. The sodium content is high due to the broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the wheat noodles is estimated at approximately 52 (low-to-moderate), but the large carbohydrate load per serving results in a high glycemic load, requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally considered halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) exists for this specific composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy proxies for the individual components (beef noodle soup, roasted chicken leg, beverage). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled wheat noodles, GI 52). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. This record represents a composite set meal sold at Lan Ting Xu Lanzhou Beef Noodles (兰亭序) on field survey data (Lazada One outlet). The set meal price is SGD 19.90. 2. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated from weighted USDA SR Legacy proxies: beef noodle soup component (~53% by weight, USDA SR Legacy 06016), roasted chicken leg component (~27% by weight, USDA SR Legacy 05170), and beverage component (~20% by weight, negligible macros, treated as water/tea). 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy proxy sources used for this composite estimation. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy beef noodle soup proxy (SR Legacy 06016) and cannot be reliably estimated for the composite. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy beef noodle soup proxy and cannot be reliably estimated for the composite. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled wheat noodles); direct GI measurement for Lanzhou hand-pulled noodles is not available in approved sources. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 79.5 g carbohydrate per 750 g serving) / 100 ≈ 41.3–41.9. 8. Beverage type is unspecified in the field survey listing; nutrient values assume a low-calorie or zero-calorie beverage (Chinese tea or water); if a sweetened soft drink is included, energy_kcal, sugar_g, and carbohydrate_g per 100 g would be higher. 9. Halal status is marked true based on the restaurant's apparent positioning as a beef and chicken noodle specialist without pork items; this should be verified against the restaurant's official halal certification. 10. This record should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo publishes a direct entry for Lanzhou beef noodle set meals.
